critic_raw: The design uses '18-24 months of production IT support tickets.' At the lower bound (18 months), training covers only 12 months with 3-month validation and 3-month test. At 24 months, training covers 18 months. This 6-month variance in training size may significantly affect model quality — a 50% larger training set could give the sequence model substantially more signal. Are results dependent on which end of the data range is available?
defender_raw: Round 1: The 18-24 month range acknowledges that different organizations will have different amounts of historical data. The design is robust to this variation because: (1) both models are trained on the same amount of data; (2) with fewer training months, both models would perform worse but the comparison is still valid; (3) the design should report results with the actual data duration, enabling replication with different data volumes. If a minimum 18-month requirement is met, the experiment is valid. The scope statement would note that results are conditional on the specific training data duration.

rounds.critic_followup: If the experiment uses only 18 months (minimum), the training set is 12 months. For an IT support system that has experienced product releases every 6 months, 12 months of training may cover only 2-3 product cycles — potentially insufficient diversity. Should the design specify a minimum training duration based on product release cycles?
rounds.defender_followup: This is a valid consideration for a specific deployment. The design uses 'temporal split: training (months 1-12)' as a fixed proportion (2/3 of 18 months). For an IT company with 6-month release cycles, 12 months covers 2 cycles — adequate for learning cross-cycle patterns. The vocabulary overlap check explicitly tests whether the training and test vocabularies align. If only 1-2 product cycles are in training and a new major release occurs in the test window, the vocabulary drift check would flag this. The design includes this diagnostic; the experimenter should document the product cycle context. This is an implementation detail, not a design flaw.

rounds.critic_followup: The design reports results with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling. For a 3-month test set at a company with, say, 1,000 tickets per month, the test set has ~3,000 tickets. Bootstrap resampling of 3,000 tickets is adequate for Recall@10 estimation. But if the company has only 100 tickets per month, the test set has 300 tickets — bootstrap CIs would be very wide. Has the design specified minimum sample size requirements for the test set?
rounds.defender_followup: The design specifies '18-24 months of production IT support tickets' without a minimum ticket count. For low-volume helpdesks (100 tickets/month), 300 test tickets would give wide CIs — but the comparison would still be valid; the CIs would honestly reflect uncertainty. If the CIs are too wide to distinguish the models, this is a power finding: the experiment cannot reliably compare models at this data volume. The design should specify a minimum ticket count for adequate statistical power — e.g., at least 500 test set tickets. This is a missing specification that would strengthen the design, but it does not invalidate the comparison protocol.

adjudication_raw: Two rounds on training data volume variance and test set minimum size. Both resolved: both models share the same training duration; wide CIs are honest results for low-volume helpdesks; minimum sample size specification would strengthen the design but is not required for validity. Verdict: defense_wins.
